levels.intro: A1 grammar unlocks the survival core of Croatian: introducing yourself, describing people and things in the nominative, using the accusative for what you eat, buy and see, and talking about the past (perfekt) and future (futur I) with the most common verb classes.

topics.title: The alphabet and sounds (abeceda i glasovi)
topics.summary: Croatian uses a 30-letter Latin alphabet where every letter is always pronounced the same way, so once you learn the sounds you can read anything aloud. Five letters carry diacritics: č (hard 'ch' as in 'church'), ć (soft 'ch', tongue closer to the palate), š ('sh'), ž ('s' in 'pleasure') and đ (soft 'j' as in 'jeans'). Three sounds are written with two letters but count as one: dž (hard 'j'), lj ('lli' in 'million') and nj ('ny' in 'canyon'). Stress never falls on the last syllable of a word; in most words it is on the first syllable. The letter r can act as a vowel between consonants (trg, prst, Hrvatska).
tables:
Letter  Sound (approx.)        Example
------  ---------------------  ----------------
c       ts in 'cats'           cesta (road)
č       hard ch in 'church'    čaj (tea)
ć       soft ch (palatal)      kuća (house)
dž      hard j in 'jam'        džep (pocket)
đ       soft j in 'jeans'      đak (pupil)
h       kh, as in 'loch'       hvala (thanks)
j       y in 'yes'             jutro (morning)
lj      lli in 'million'       ljeto (summer)
nj      ny in 'canyon'         knjiga (book)
š       sh in 'shoe'           škola (school)
ž       s in 'pleasure'        žena (woman)
r       can be a vowel         trg, prst, Hrvatska

topics.title: Noun gender and the nominative (rod imenica i nominativ)
topics.summary: Every Croatian noun is masculine, feminine or neuter, and you can usually tell from the nominative singular ending: masculine nouns end in a consonant (grad, stol), feminine nouns end in -a (žena, kuća), and neuter nouns end in -o or -e (selo, more). A smaller group of feminine nouns ends in a consonant (noć, ljubav) — these follow the i-declension covered later. Two sound rules affect many forms: the 'fleeting a' (nepostojano a) drops from the last syllable when an ending is added (momak → momci, pas → psi), and sibilarization changes k, g, h to c, z, s before the ending -i (momak → momci, bubreg → bubrezi). Most one-syllable masculine nouns take a long plural in -ovi/-evi (grad → gradovi, muž → muževi; -evi appears after soft consonants like č, ž, š, đ, ć, j, lj, nj).
tables:
Gender             Nom. sg.        Nom. pl.         Examples
-----------------  --------------  ---------------  -----------------------
masculine          -ø (consonant)  -i               student -> studenti
masc. (1 syllable) -ø              -ovi / -evi      grad -> gradovi
                                                    muž  -> muževi
feminine (-a)      -a              -e               žena -> žene
feminine (i-decl.) -ø (consonant)  -i               noć  -> noći
neuter             -o / -e         -a               selo -> sela
                                                    more -> mora

Sound changes in the plural
---------------------------------------------
sibilarization  k -> c   momak  -> momci
(before -i)     g -> z   bubreg -> bubrezi
                h -> s   orah   -> orasi
fleeting a      momak -> momka -> momci
(drops before   pas   -> psa   -> psi
an ending)      vrabac -> vrapca -> vrapci

topics.title: Personal pronouns and 'to be' (lične zamjenice i glagol biti)
topics.summary: The verb biti (to be) has three sets of present forms: long (stressed) forms used in short answers and for emphasis (Jesam!), short (clitic) forms used in normal sentences (Ja sam student), and negative forms (nisam). The short forms are enclitics — they can never stand first in a sentence; they lean on the first stressed word (Student sam). Because verb endings show the person, subject pronouns (ja, ti, on...) are normally dropped unless you want contrast or emphasis. The reflexive verb zvati se (to be called) is how you give your name: Zovem se Ana; the clitic se follows the same second-position rule.
tables:
Person        Long     Short   Negative
------------  -------  ------  --------
ja            jesam    sam     nisam
ti            jesi     si      nisi
on/ona/ono    jest     je      nije
mi            jesmo    smo     nismo
vi            jeste    ste     niste
oni/one/ona   jesu     su      nisu

zvati se (present)
------------------
ja      zovem se     mi   zovemo se
ti      zoveš se     vi   zovete se
on/ona  zove se      oni  zovu se
Word order: Zovem se Ana. / Kako se zoveš?

examples.gloss: Are you a student? — I am.

topics.title: The present tense: -am, -im, -em classes (prezent)
topics.summary: Croatian has one present tense, and every verb belongs to one of three ending classes: -am (most verbs in -ati: gledati → gledam), -im (verbs in -iti and -jeti: govoriti → govorim, živjeti → živim) and -em (verbs whose present stem differs from the infinitive: pisati → pišem, ići → idem). The endings themselves are completely regular — only the stem must be learned. Verbs in -ovati/-evati/-ivati switch to -ujem (putovati → putujem, kupovati → kupujem), while verbs in -irati stay in the -am class (studirati → studiram). A few everyday verbs are irregular and must be memorized: jesti → jedem, piti → pijem, ići → idem, slati → šaljem, pisati → pišem. Negation puts ne directly before the verb (ne radim); only imati has a fused negative, nemati (nemam).
tables:
-am class      -im class      -em class
          gledati        govoriti       pisati
          (to watch)     (to speak)     (to write)
--------  -------------  -------------  ----------
ja        gledam         govorim        pišem
ti        gledaš         govoriš        pišeš
on/ona    gleda          govori         piše
mi        gledamo        govorimo       pišemo
vi        gledate        govorite       pišete
oni       gledaju        govore         pišu

Infinitive pattern      Present        Example
----------------------  -------------  --------------------
-ati                    -am            gledati -> gledam
-irati                  -iram          studirati -> studiram
-iti / -jeti            -im            govoriti -> govorim
                                       živjeti  -> živim
-ovati / -evati / -ivati  -ujem        putovati -> putujem
                                       kazivati -> kazujem
irregular stems         -em            jesti -> jedem
                                       piti  -> pijem
                                       ići   -> idem
                                       slati -> šaljem

topics.title: Numbers up to a trillion (brojevi do bilijun)
topics.summary: Croatian numbers are built regularly: 11-19 add -naest (jedanaest, dvanaest), tens add -deset (dvadeset, trideset), and larger units are sto (100), tisuća (1,000), milijun and bilijun. The number jedan agrees like an adjective (jedan grad, jedna žena, jedno selo), and dva has a feminine form dvije. After dva/dvije, tri and četiri, the noun takes a special paucal form identical to the genitive singular (dva grada, tri žene); after pet and higher, it takes the genitive plural (pet gradova, šest žena). This agreement rule applies to the last word of a compound number: dvadeset jedan student, dvadeset dva studenta, dvadeset pet studenata.
tables:
1 jedan/jedna/jedno   11 jedanaest    10  deset
2 dva/dvije           12 dvanaest     20  dvadeset
3 tri                 13 trinaest     30  trideset
4 četiri              14 četrnaest    40  četrdeset
5 pet                 15 petnaest     50  pedeset
6 šest                16 šesnaest     60  šezdeset
7 sedam               17 sedamnaest   70  sedamdeset
8 osam                18 osamnaest    80  osamdeset
9 devet               19 devetnaest   90  devedeset
100 sto    200 dvjesto    1 000 tisuća
1 000 000 milijun          1 000 000 000 000 bilijun

After 1          nom. sg.   jedan grad, jedna žena
After 2, 3, 4    gen. sg.   dva grada, dvije žene
After 5+         gen. pl.   pet gradova, šest žena

topics.title: The accusative case (akuzativ)
topics.summary: The accusative marks the direct object — the thing directly affected by the verb (Čitam knjigu) — and is also used after the prepositions u and na for motion toward a place (Idem u školu, Idemo na more) and after po ('to fetch') and za ('for'). Feminine nouns in -a change -a to -u (žena → ženu); neuter nouns and inanimate masculine nouns look exactly like the nominative (Vidim grad); but animate masculine nouns (people and animals) take -a, borrowing the genitive form (Vidim prijatelja). In the plural, masculine nouns take -e, feminine nouns -e, i-declension nouns -i, and neuter nouns -a.
tables:
Accusative singular
Gender             Ending      Example
-----------------  ----------  ----------------------
m. inanimate       = nom.      grad  -> Vidim grad
m. animate         -a          prijatelj -> Vidim prijatelja
f. (-a)            -u          žena  -> Vidim ženu
f. (i-declension)  = nom.      noć   -> Čekam noć
neuter             = nom.      selo  -> Vidim selo

Accusative plural
m. -e   gradove, prijatelje
f. -e   žene
f. -i   noći
n. -a   sela

Prepositions with the accusative (motion / purpose)
---------------------------------------------------
u  + A   into        Idem u grad.
na + A   onto, to    Idemo na more.
po + A   to fetch    Idem po kruh.
za + A   for         Ovo je poklon za tebe.

topics.title: Possessives, demonstratives and descriptive adjectives (posvojne i pokazne zamjenice, opisni pridjevi)
topics.summary: Possessive pronouns (moj, tvoj, njegov, njezin, naš, vaš, njihov) and descriptive adjectives agree with the noun they describe in gender and number: moj brat, moja sestra, moje selo; velik grad, velika kuća, veliko more. Croatian has a three-way demonstrative system based on distance: ovaj (near me), taj (near you), onaj (over there); their plurals are irregular — ovaj → ovi, taj → ti, onaj → oni. To say that something belongs to a person, Croatian prefers a possessive adjective made from the name or noun: -ov/-ev for masculine possessors (Markov auto, Hrvojev brat), -in for feminine (Anin stan, sestrin muž); this pattern is used for living possessors, while for inanimate things you use the genitive or a relational adjective instead (vrata kuće, gradski trg).
tables:
Possessive     m.        f.        n.
-------------  --------  --------  --------
my             moj       moja      moje
your (sg.)     tvoj      tvoja     tvoje
his            njegov    njegova   njegovo
her            njezin    njezina   njezino
our            naš       naša      naše
your (pl./V)   vaš       vaša      vaše
their          njihov    njihova   njihovo

Demonstrative   m. sg.  f. sg.  n. sg.  m. pl.  f. pl.  n. pl.
--------------  ------  ------  ------  ------  ------  ------
this (near me)  ovaj    ova     ovo     ovi     ove     ova
that (near you) taj     ta      to      ti      te      ta
that (yonder)   onaj    ona     ono     oni     one     ona

Possessive adjectives from persons
----------------------------------
masc. possessor  -ov / -ev   Marko -> Markov stan
                             Hrvoje -> Hrvojev auto
fem. possessor   -in         Ana -> Anin brat
                             sestra -> sestrin muž

topics.title: Modal verbs (modalni glagoli: morati, smjeti, trebati, htjeti, moći)
topics.summary: Modal verbs combine with an infinitive to express obligation (morati — must), permission (smjeti — may), need (trebati — need to), wish (htjeti — want) and ability (moći — can): Moram raditi, Smijem li ući? Morati, smjeti and trebati conjugate regularly (moram, smijem, trebam), but htjeti and moći are irregular: hoću, hoćeš... hoće with the fused negative neću, and mogu, možeš... mogu (note 1st sg. and 3rd pl. in -u). Infinitives end in -ti or -ći (raditi, ići, doći, reći); after a modal the infinitive never changes.
tables:
htjeti    negative   moći
-------  --------  ---------  -------
ja       hoću      neću       mogu
ti       hoćeš     nećeš      možeš
on/ona   hoće      neće       može
mi       hoćemo    nećemo     možemo
vi       hoćete    nećete     možete
oni      hoće      neće       mogu

morati -> moram (must)     smjeti -> smijem (may)
trebati -> trebam (need)   + infinitive (-ti / -ći)

examples.target: Moram učiti, ne mogu doći na kavu.
examples.gloss: I have to study, I can't come for coffee.

examples.target: Smijem li ovdje parkirati?
examples.gloss: May I park here?

examples.target: Hoćeš li ići s nama u kino?

topics.title: The past tense (perfekt)
topics.summary: The perfekt is the all-purpose past tense in spoken Croatian. It is formed from the present of biti (short forms: sam, si, je...) plus the active past participle, which agrees with the subject in gender and number: radio sam (a man speaking), radila sam (a woman), radili smo (we). The participle is made by replacing the infinitive -ti with -o/-la/-lo (sg.) and -li/-le/-la (pl.): raditi → radio/radila. Verbs in -ći and a few others have irregular participles that must be learned: ići → išao/išla, reći → rekao/rekla, moći → mogao/mogla. Because sam/si/je are clitics, they stand in second position (Jučer sam radio); in the negative they merge with ne and can stand first (Nisam radio). With reflexive verbs, the 3rd person singular je is dropped: On se smijao (not 'se je').
tables:
Past participle (glagolski pridjev radni)
            m.        f.        n.
----------  --------  --------  --------
singular    radio     radila    radilo
plural      radili    radile    radila

Formation: infinitive minus -ti + -o/-la/-lo/-li/-le/-la
gledati -> gledao      govoriti -> govorio
živjeti -> živio       učiti    -> učio

Irregular participles
biti  -> bio,   bila,  bilo   | bili,  bile,  bila
ići   -> išao,  išla,  išlo   | išli,  išle,  išla
reći  -> rekao, rekla, reklo  | rekli, rekle, rekla
moći  -> mogao, mogla, moglo  | mogli, mogle, mogla

perfekt = biti (short) + participle
Ja sam radio/radila.        Nisam radio/radila.
Ti si radio/radila.         Jesi li radio/radila?
On je radio. Ona je radila. On se smijao. (no 'je' with se)
Mi smo radili/radile.       Radili smo cijeli dan.

topics.title: The future tense (futur prvi)
topics.summary: Futur I is formed with the clitic present of htjeti (ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će) plus the infinitive. Word order determines spelling: if the infinitive comes first, verbs in -ti drop the final i and the clitic follows (Radit ću, Gledat ćemo — pronounced as one word), while verbs in -ći keep their full form (Doći ću). If anything else starts the sentence, the clitic comes second and the infinitive keeps -ti (Sutra ću raditi, Ja ću doći). The negative uses the stressed forms neću, nećeš... which can stand first (Neću raditi), and questions use Hoćeš li...? or the colloquial Hoćeš li doći? With reflexive verbs, se joins the clitic cluster: Vidjet ćemo se sutra.
tables:
Person   Clitic   Infinitive-first     Other-first
-------  -------  -------------------  ------------------
ja       ću       Radit ću.            Sutra ću raditi.
ti       ćeš      Radit ćeš.           Ti ćeš raditi.
on/ona   će       Radit će.            On će raditi.
mi       ćemo     Radit ćemo.          Mi ćemo raditi.
vi       ćete     Radit ćete.          Vi ćete raditi.
oni      će       Radit će.            Oni će raditi.

-ći verbs keep the full infinitive: Doći ću. Ići ćemo.
negative: neću, nećeš, neće, nećemo, nećete, neće + inf.
question: Hoćeš li doći? / Hoćete li raditi?

topics.title: The dative and indirect objects (dativ i neizravni objekt)
topics.summary: The dative marks the indirect object — the person to whom something is given, said or sent: Dajem prijatelju knjigu (I give my friend a book). Singular endings: masculine and neuter -u (prijatelju, djetetu), feminine -i (ženi, sestri; with sibilarization: ruka → ruci); plural -ima/-ama for all genders. Some common verbs govern the dative directly (glagoli s dativnom rekcijom): pomagati komu (to help), vjerovati komu (to believe), telefonirati komu (to phone), čestitati komu (to congratulate), zahvaliti komu (to thank). The verb boljeti works differently — the body part is the subject and the person in pain is in the accusative: Boli me glava (my head hurts), Bole me leđa. When a sentence has both objects, the direct object is accusative and the recipient dative: Pišem baki pismo.
tables:
Dative endings
                sg.        pl.
--------------  ---------  -----------
m.              -u         -ima
                prijatelju prijateljima
n.              -u         -ima
                djetetu    djeci*
f. (-a)         -i         -ama
                ženi       ženama
f. (i-decl.)    -i         -ima
                noći       noćima
sibilarization in f. sg.: ruka -> ruci, noga -> nozi, knjiga -> knjizi

Dative-rection verbs
pomagati komu     Pomažem majci.
vjerovati komu    Vjerujem ti.
telefonirati komu Telefoniram bratu.
čestitati komu    Čestitam ti rođendan.
zahvaliti komu    Zahvaljujem vam.

boljeti: body part = subject, person = accusative
Boli me glava.  Bole me leđa.  Boli ga zub.

topics.title: I-declension nouns and pluralia tantum (imenice i-deklinacije, pluralia tantum)
topics.summary: A group of feminine nouns ends in a consonant and follows the i-declension: noć, stvar, ljubav, riječ, and all abstract nouns in -ost (radost, mladost, mogućnost). Their pattern is very simple — genitive, dative and locative singular and nominative plural all end in -i (noći). Some nouns exist only in the plural (pluralia tantum) and always take plural agreement even for one object: neuter vrata (door), kola (car/cart), leđa (back), usta (mouth), and feminine hlače (trousers), naočale (glasses), novine (newspaper), škare (scissors). The noun posao (job) has an irregular stem: the -ao becomes -l- before endings (posao, posla, poslu; plural poslovi).

i-declension: noć (f.)          -ost nouns: radost (f.)
      sg.     pl.                     sg.       pl.
N     noć     noći               N    radost    radosti
G     noći    noći               G    radosti   radosti
D     noći    noćima             D    radosti   radostima
A     noć     noći               A    radost    radosti
L     noći    noćima             L    radosti   radostima
I     noći/noću  noćima          I    radošću   radostima

posao (m.): posao, posla, poslu, posao, poslu, poslom
plural:      poslovi, poslova, poslovima, poslove, ...

Pluralia tantum (always plural)

          novine (newspaper), škare (scissors)

topics.title: Imperative basics (imperativ glagola na -iti, -ati)
topics.summary: The imperative gives commands and instructions and has forms for ti, mi and vi. It is built from the 3rd person plural present: if that form ends in -aju, the imperative ending is -aj (gledaju → gledaj!, gledajte!); if it ends in -e or -u after a consonant, the ending is -i (govore → govori!, govorite!; uče → uči!). The vi-form doubles as the polite form for one person (Izvolite, uđite!). The negative imperative uses nemoj/nemojte + infinitive for polite prohibition (Nemoj kasniti!) or ne + imperative (Ne kasni!).
tables:
gledati (-ati)   govoriti (-iti)
-------  ---------------  ----------------
(ti)     gledaj!          govori!
(mi)     gledajmo!        govorimo!
(vi)     gledajte!        govorite!

Rule: from 3rd pl. present
-aju -> -aj(te)    gledaju -> gledaj!
-e/-u -> -i(te)    govore  -> govori!  pišu -> piši!

negative: nemoj + inf. / ne + imperative
Nemoj kasniti! = Ne kasni!

examples.target: Govori sporije, molim te!
examples.gloss: Speak more slowly, please!

examples.target: Gledajte ovu fotografiju.
examples.gloss: Look at this photograph. (polite/plural)

levels.intro: A2 grammar completes the case system — full declension of nouns, adjectives and pronouns in all seven cases — and adds comparison, the conditional, verbal aspect and the clitic-order rules you need for natural, correctly built sentences.

topics.title: Full noun declension: the three classes (sklonidba imenica)
topics.summary: Croatian nouns decline through seven cases (nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, vocative, locative, instrumental) in singular and plural, and every noun follows one of three patterns: the a-declension (masculine and neuter: grad, prijatelj, selo), the e-declension (nouns in -a, mostly feminine: žena) and the i-declension (feminine nouns in a consonant: noć). Dative and locative are always identical, and in the plural dative, locative and instrumental share one form (-ima/-ama). Masculine instrumental singular is -om after hard consonants and -em after soft ones (č, ć, đ, ž, š, j, lj, nj: prijateljem, mužem). Animate masculine nouns take the genitive form in the accusative singular (Vidim prijatelja), while inanimate ones match the nominative.

topics.title: Special noun patterns: stem extensions, foreign nouns, dio (posebne sklonidbe)
topics.summary: Some neuter nouns extend their stem outside the nominative/accusative singular with -(e)n- or -(e)t-: ime → imena (G sg.), rame → ramena; dijete → djeteta, tele → teleta (and the plural of dijete is the collective djeca). Foreign nouns ending in -y, -i, -u, -o, -e decline like masculine nouns by inserting -j- or adding endings to the full form: taksi → taksija, intervju → intervjua, auto → auta, finale → finala; nouns in -io insert -j-: radio → radija, studio → studija. The noun dio (part) changes its stem to dijel-: dio, dijela, dijelu, plural dijelovi — the same -io → -jel- alternation as posao → posla.

G sg  imena             djeteta
D sg  imenu             djetetu
I sg  imenom            djetetom
N pl  imena             djeca (collective!)

topics.title: Adjective declension (sklonidba pridjeva)
topics.summary: Adjectives agree with their noun in gender, number and case, so they decline too. Masculine and neuter share most endings (-og(a) in genitive, -om(u/e) in dative and locative, -im in instrumental), while feminine follows the e-declension pattern (-e, -oj, -u, -om). In the masculine accusative singular the animacy rule applies: inanimate matches the nominative (Vidim velik grad), animate matches the genitive (Vidim dobrog prijatelja). Adjectives like dobar show the fleeting a (dobar → dobrog). Croatian also distinguishes indefinite (dobar čovjek) and definite (dobri čovjek) masculine nominative forms — the definite form in -i is used with known referents and always after demonstratives (taj dobri čovjek). Relational adjectives in -ski, -ški, -čki, -ćki (gradski, zagrebački) describe type or origin, only have the definite form, and never form comparatives.

topics.title: Demonstratives, possessives and reflexive svoj in all cases (sklonidba zamjenica; povratno-posvojna zamjenica)
topics.summary: Demonstrative (ovaj, taj, onaj) and possessive (moj, tvoj, naš...) pronouns decline like definite adjectives: taj → tog(a), tom(u), tim; moj → mog(a)/mojeg(a), mom(u)/mojem(u), mojim. The reflexive-possessive pronoun svoj means 'one's own' and must be used whenever the possessor is the subject of the clause, regardless of person: Čitam svoju knjigu (my own), On čita svoju knjigu (his own). Using moj or njegov instead changes or blurs the meaning — Njegov auto is someone else's car, svoj auto is the subject's own. Svoj declines exactly like moj and is never used in the nominative describing the subject itself.
tables:
taj (that), singular            moj / svoj, masculine sg.
Case  m.        n.     f.        Case  form
----  --------  -----  ----      ----  -----------------

G     tog(a)    tog(a) te        G     mog(a) / svog(a)
D     tom(u)    tom(u) toj       D     mom(u) / svom(u)
A     =N/G      to     tu        A     = N (inanim.) / G (anim.)
L     tom(e)    tom(e) toj       L     mom(e) / svom(e)
I     tim       tim    tom       I     mojim / svojim
(f.: svoja -> svoje, svojoj, svoju, svojoj, svojom)

svoj vs. moj/njegov
subject = possessor  -> svoj
Ja čitam svoju knjigu.   (my own book)
On vozi svoj auto.       (his own car)
subject != possessor -> moj/tvoj/njegov...

topics.title: Personal pronouns in all cases and clitic order (lične zamjenice; redoslijed enklitika)
topics.summary: Personal pronouns have stressed (naglašeni) and clitic (nenaglašeni) forms in the genitive, dative and accusative: mene/me, meni/mi, njega/ga, njoj/joj. Clitics are the default; stressed forms are used after prepositions, for contrast, and at the start of an answer (Koga vidiš? — Njega.). All clitics gather in second position in the clause, in a fixed internal order: the question particle li, then verbal auxiliaries (sam, si, smo, ste, su; ću, ćeš...; bih, bi...), then the dative clitic, then the accusative/genitive clitic, then se — and the auxiliary je always comes last of all. Example: Dala mi ga je (She gave it to me). This order never varies, so it is worth memorizing as a template.
tables:
Personal pronouns: stressed / clitic
        G            D            A
ja      mene/me      meni/mi      mene/me
ti      tebe/te      tebi/ti      tebe/te
on/ono  njega/ga     njemu/mu     njega/ga
ona     nje/je       njoj/joj     nju/ju (je)
mi      nas/nas      nama/nam     nas/nas
vi      vas/vas      vama/vam     vas/vas
oni     njih/ih      njima/im     njih/ih
(after prepositions: stressed form  -> kod mene, za njega)

Clitic order template (2nd position in clause)
 li -> aux -> DAT -> ACC/GEN -> se -> je
(1)   (2)    (3)      (4)     (5)   (6)

Jesi li mu ga dao?        li + DAT + ACC
Dala mi ga je.            DAT + ACC + je last
Sjećam ga se.             GEN + se
Predstavit ću ti je.      aux + DAT + ACC
Ona mu se nasmijala.      DAT + se ('je' dropped with se)

topics.title: More present-tense classes (prezentske vrste)
topics.summary: A2 adds the remaining common present-stem patterns, all still using the regular -em endings. Verbs in -nuti conjugate with -nem (krenuti → krenem); doći and its family give dođem, dođeš. Many verbs in -ati soften their final consonant before -jem endings (iotation): pisati → pišem, kazati → kažem, plakati → plačem. Short vowel-stem verbs like čuti simply insert j (čujem, pijem), and verbs in -vati switch to -jem (davati → dajem, poznavati → poznajem). Consonant-stem verbs include the peći type (pečem, but 3rd pl. peku) and the -sti → -dem type (jesti → jedem, krasti → kradem). The stem must be learned with each verb, but once you know the 1st person singular, the whole paradigm follows automatically.
tables:
Pattern            Model        Present (ja, ti, ... oni)
-----------------  -----------  --------------------------------
-(n)uti -> -nem    krenuti      krenem, kreneš, krene,
                                krenemo, krenete, krenu
doći    -> dođem   doći         dođem, dođeš ... dođu
-ati -> -jem       pisati       pišem, pišeš ... pišu
(iotation)         kazati       kažem ... kažu
                   plakati      plačem ... plaču
čuti type          čuti         čujem, čuješ ... čuju
-vati -> -jem      davati       dajem, daješ ... daju
                   poznavati    poznajem ... poznaju
peći type          peći         pečem, pečeš ... peku (3. pl!)
-sti -> -dem       jesti        jedem, jedeš ... jedu
                   krasti       kradem ... kradu

topics.title: Verbal aspect: perfective and imperfective (glagolski vid)
topics.summary: Almost every Croatian verb comes in a pair: an imperfective verb (nesvršeni) for ongoing, repeated or unfinished actions, and a perfective verb (svršeni) for single completed actions — pisati/napisati, kupovati/kupiti, dolaziti/doći. English hides this distinction in tenses ('was writing' vs 'wrote'); Croatian builds it into the verb itself, so choosing the right member of the pair matters in every tense. Perfective verbs cannot form a true present with present meaning — Napišem pismo only works in dependent clauses or with future reference; for 'I am writing' you must say Pišem. In the perfekt, aspect distinguishes Pisao sam pismo (I was writing / used to write) from Napisao sam pismo (I finished writing it). Dictionaries list verbs as pairs, and it is best to learn them together from the start.
tables:
Imperfective (process)     Perfective (completed)
-------------------------  ----------------------
pisati    (be writing)     napisati  (write, finish)
čitati    (be reading)     pročitati (read through)
kupovati  (be buying)      kupiti    (buy)
dolaziti  (be coming)      doći      (come, arrive)
gledati   (be watching)    pogledati (take a look)
učiti     (study)          naučiti   (learn, master)

Use imperfective for:  process, habit, duration
  Svaki dan pišem mailove.  Pisao sam cijelo jutro.
Use perfective for:    one whole completed event
  Napisao sam mail i poslao ga.

topics.title: Comparative and superlative (komparativ i superlativ)
topics.summary: Most adjectives form the comparative with -iji (star → stariji, pametan → pametniji). Shorter adjectives take -ji, which fuses with the final consonant (iotation): mlad → mlađi, jak → jači, skup → skuplji, tih → tiši. Exactly three adjectives take -ši: lijep → ljepši, lak → lakši, mek → mekši. Four key adjectives are suppletive and must be memorized: dobar → bolji, loš → gori, velik → veći, malen/mali → manji. The superlative is always naj- + comparative (najbolji, najstariji). Comparatives and superlatives decline like definite adjectives (boljeg, boljem, s boljim). Comparison uses od + genitive or nego: Ivan je stariji od Marka / stariji nego Marko. Adverbs compare with the neuter form: dobro → bolje → najbolje, mnogo → više, malo → manje, loše → gore.

Pattern       Positive   Comparative   Superlative
------------  ---------  ------------  ------------

              pametan    pametniji     najpametniji

              skup       skuplji       najskuplji
              tih        tiši          najtiši
-ši (only 3)  lijep      ljepši        najljepši
              lak        lakši         najlakši
              mek        mekši         najmekši
irregular     dobar      bolji         najbolji
              loš        gori          najgori
              velik      veći          najveći
              mali       manji         najmanji

topics.title: The conditional (kondicional prvi)
topics.summary: Kondicional I is formed with the aorist of biti (bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi) plus the past participle: Rado bih došao (I would gladly come). These auxiliary forms are clitics and stand in second position; in careful standard Croatian the persons are distinguished (bismo, biste), even though colloquial speech levels them all to bi. The negative puts ne before the auxiliary (ne bih rekao) and the question uses inversion with li (Biste li htjeli kavu?). The conditional expresses wishes (Htio bih...), polite requests (Biste li mi pomogli?), possibility, and conditions — both realizable and unrealizable — often introduced by kad or da: Kad bih imao vremena, putovao bih.

Person   Aux.    Example
-------  ------  ---------------------------
ja       bih     Ja bih došao. / Došao bih.
ti       bi      Ti bi došao.
on/ona   bi      On bi došao.
mi       bismo   Mi bismo došli.
vi       biste   Vi biste došli.
oni      bi      Oni bi došli.

negative:  Ne bih to rekao.
question:  Bi li došao?  Biste li htjeli kavu?
meanings:  wish        Htjela bih sladoled.
           politeness  Biste li mi pomogli?
           possibility To bi moglo biti točno.

topics.title: Futur II and time/condition clauses (futur drugi; zavisne rečenice s kad, ako, dok)
topics.summary: Futur II is formed with budem, budeš, bude, budemo, budete, budu plus the past participle (budem radio). It never stands alone: it appears in dependent clauses after kad (when), ako (if) and dok (while/until) to mark a future action that precedes or accompanies the main future action: Kad budem imao vremena, nazvat ću te. With perfective verbs, the bare present usually replaces futur II in these clauses (Kad dođem, javit ću ti se) — that is the normal, most frequent pattern; futur II is chiefly needed with imperfective verbs. The main clause keeps futur I. Note the meaning of dok ne + perfective: 'until' (Čekat ću dok ne dođeš).
tables:
futur II = budem + participle
ja  budem radio/radila    mi  budemo radili/radile
ti  budeš radio/radila    vi  budete radili/radile
on  bude radio            oni budu radili

Dependent clause (kad/ako/dok)     Main clause (futur I)
--------------------------------   ---------------------
Kad budem imao vremena,            nazvat ću te.
Ako budeš učio,                    položit ćeš ispit.
Dok budeš spavao,                  ja ću raditi.
perfective verb -> present instead of futur II:
Kad dođem kući,                    javit ću ti se.
Ako vidiš Anu,                     pozdravi je.

topics.title: Uses of the genitive (vremenski, posvojni i objasnidbeni genitiv)
topics.summary: Beyond 'of' and quantities, A2 focuses on three genitive functions. The temporal genitive puts time expressions directly into the genitive with no preposition: prošle godine (last year), svakog dana (every day), jednog jutra (one morning). The possessive genitive expresses belonging when a possessive adjective is impossible — typically when the possessor carries a modifier: auto moga brata (my brother's car), kuća stare gospođe. The explanatory (objasnidbeni) genitive specifies what something consists of or refers to: osjećaj sreće (a feeling of happiness), problem nezaposlenosti, miris mora. The genitive is also required after quantity words (puno, malo, nekoliko + G pl.) and many prepositions (iz, od, do, kod, bez, blizu, pored).

               jednog jutra       one morning

(modified      stan naših susjeda our neighbours' flat

               miris mora         smell of the sea

topics.title: Impersonal sentences (impersonalne rečenice)
topics.summary: Croatian often builds sentences without a nominative subject. With states and feelings, the verb or adjective stands in the neuter 3rd person singular and the person appears in the dative: Hladno mi je (I am cold), Dosadno joj je (she is bored). Necessity and possibility use treba and može with an infinitive or a se-construction: Treba učiti (one must study), Može se ući (one may enter), Ovdje se ne puši (no smoking here). Past tense of all these takes the neuter singular: Bilo je hladno, Trebalo je raditi. These patterns are extremely common in everyday speech and signs, and English 'it/one/you' subjects all map onto them.
tables:
Pattern                 Example              English
----------------------  -------------------  -------------------
adj. (n.) + je + DAT    Hladno mi je.        I am cold.
                        Dosadno joj je.      She is bored.
treba + inf.            Treba puno raditi.   One must work a lot.
može se + inf./verb-se  Može se ući.         One may enter.
                        Ovdje se ne puši.    No smoking here.
past: neuter sg.        Bilo je hladno.      It was cold.
                        Trebalo je učiti.    One had to study.

topics.title: Comparing adverbs (komparativ i superlativ priloga)
topics.summary: Adverbs of manner look like the neuter singular of the adjective (brzo, dobro, loše) and compare using the neuter comparative form: brzo → brže, često → češće, dobro → bolje, loše → gore, mnogo → više, malo → manje. The superlative again adds naj- (najbrže, najbolje). These comparative adverbs are everywhere in everyday speech — govori sporije (speak more slowly), dođi ranije (come earlier) — and also combine with što to mean 'as ... as possible': što prije (as soon as possible), što više (as much as possible).

malo       manje         najmanje
često      češće         najčešće

levels.intro: B1 grammar turns correct sentences into fluent Croatian: mastering aspect pairs and prefixed verb families, the passive participle, verbal nouns and adverbs, and the full range of conditional sentences lets you narrate, argue and write connected texts.

topics.title: Aspect pairs in depth (vidski parovi)
topics.summary: At B1 you systematize how aspect pairs are formed. Three patterns cover nearly all verbs: (1) a prefix turns an imperfective into a perfective (pisati → napisati, gledati → pogledati); (2) a suffix — usually -avati, -ivati or -ati — derives a new imperfective from a perfective (kupiti → kupovati, dati → davati, dogovoriti se → dogovarati se); (3) a few pairs use different stems altogether (suppletive: dolaziti/doći, govoriti/reći, uzimati/uzeti). Crucially, when a prefix adds a new meaning (pisati → opisati 'describe'), that new perfective verb gets its own derived imperfective (opisivati), creating a secondary pair. Learning verbs in these families — rather than one by one — is the fastest route to B1 vocabulary.
tables:
Pattern                    impf.           pf.
-------------------------  --------------  --------------
1. prefix (impf. -> pf.)   pisati          napisati
   (same meaning,          čitati          pročitati
    completed)             gledati         pogledati
                           raditi          uraditi
2. suffix (pf. -> impf.)   kupiti          kupovati
   -avati/-ivati/-ati      dati            davati
                           dogovoriti se   dogovarati se
                           ostaviti        ostavljati
3. suppletive /            dolaziti        doći
   stem change             govoriti        reći
                           uzimati         uzeti

New meaning -> secondary pair:
pisati -> opisati (describe) -> opisivati (impf.)
       -> zapisati (note down) -> zapisivati (impf.)

topics.title: Verb families with prefixes (glagoli u kontekstu: pisati, gledati, staviti...)
topics.summary: Croatian prefixes work like English phrasal-verb particles: each prefix adds a predictable shade of meaning to a base verb. From pisati: opisati (describe), zapisati (note down), potpisati (sign), prepisati (copy); from gledati: pogledati (take a look), pregledati (examine), razgledati (sightsee); from staviti: dostaviti (deliver), predstaviti (introduce), nastaviti (continue), ostaviti (leave behind), sastaviti (assemble), postaviti (set up); from stati: postati (become), ostati (stay), prestati (stop), ustati (get up). Common prefix meanings: po- (a little / begin), pre- (across, over, re-), raz- (apart, around), za- (start / fix in place), do- (reach, arrive), iz- (out), na- (onto / completion), pri- (near, join), od- (away). Learning the prefix meanings lets you decode hundreds of new verbs from context.
tables:
Base       + prefix     Meaning
---------  -----------  -----------------------
pisati     opisati      describe
(write)    zapisati     note down
           potpisati    sign
           prepisati    copy / prescribe
gledati    pogledati    take a look
(watch)    pregledati   examine, review
           razgledati   look around, sightsee
misliti    pomisliti    think of (suddenly)
(think)    izmisliti    invent, make up
           razmisliti   think over
staviti    dostaviti    deliver
(put)      predstaviti  introduce, present
           nastaviti    continue
           ostaviti     leave behind
           sastaviti    assemble, compose
           postaviti    set up, install
stati      postati      become
(stop/     ostati       stay, remain
 stand)    prestati     stop (doing)
           ustati       get up
zvati      nazvati      call (phone)
(call)     pozvati      invite
           izazvati     provoke, cause
držati     zadržati     keep, retain
(hold)     održati      hold (an event), maintain
           podržati     support
živjeti    doživjeti    experience
(live)     preživjeti   survive
pustiti    dopustiti    allow
(let)      otpustiti    dismiss, release

topics.title: Advanced present-stem classes (prezent glagola tipa stanem, sjednem, donesem)
topics.summary: B1 completes the present-stem system with the last consonant-stem patterns, all with regular -em endings. Verbs like ostati and postati form -anem (ostanem, postanem); uzeti and početi have suppletive stems uzmem and počnem. Verbs in -sti split into three types: -dnem (sjesti → sjednem, pasti → padnem), -tnem (sresti → sretnem) and -zem (gristi → grizem, vesti → vezem). Verbs in -ći of the stići type give -gnem (stignem, legnem, pobjegnem), and the -nijeti family gives -nesem (donijeti → donesem, odnijeti → odnesem). These are all perfective or mixed-aspect everyday verbs, so their present forms mostly appear in dependent clauses and futures (Kad stignem, sjednem i počnem raditi).
tables:
Pattern            Infinitive   Present (ja)
-----------------  -----------  ------------
-ati  -> -anem     ostati       ostanem
                   postati      postanem
suppletive         uzeti        uzmem
                   početi       počnem
-sti  -> -dnem     sjesti       sjednem
                   pasti        padnem
-sti  -> -tnem     sresti       sretnem
-sti  -> -zem      gristi       grizem
                   vesti        vezem
-ći   -> -gnem     stići        stignem
                   leći         legnem
                   pobjeći      pobjegnem
-ijeti -> -esem    donijeti     donesem
                   odnijeti     odnesem
                   ponijeti     ponesem

topics.title: The dative of the experiencer (nositelj stanja u dativu)
topics.summary: Croatian systematically puts the person who experiences a state in the dative while the grammatical subject is the thing or the state itself: Sviđa mi se ovaj film (literally 'this film pleases to-me'), Hladno mi je, Dosadno nam je, Žao mi je (I'm sorry), Drago mi je (pleased to meet you). The verb agrees with the nominative subject, not with the experiencer: Sviđaju mi se ove pjesme (plural verb, plural subject). This pattern also covers physical states (Zlo mi je — I feel sick, Vrti mi se — I'm dizzy) and needs (Treba mi pomoć — I need help, Nedostaje mi obitelj — I miss my family). Getting these right is a hallmark of natural Croatian, because English speakers instinctively make the experiencer the subject.
tables:
Croatian (DAT experiencer)   Literal            English
---------------------------  -----------------  -----------------
Sviđa mi se film.            pleases to-me      I like the film.
Sviđaju mi se filmovi.       (verb agrees       I like the films.
                              with subject!)
Žao mi je.                   sorry to-me        I am sorry.
Drago mi je.                 dear to-me         Pleased to meet you.
Zlo mi je.                   ill to-me          I feel sick.
Vrti mi se.                  spins to-me        I am dizzy.
Treba mi pomoć.              is-needed to-me    I need help.
Nedostaje mi obitelj.        lacks to-me        I miss my family.
Uspjelo nam je.              succeeded to-us    We succeeded.

topics.title: Instrumental of consonant-final feminine nouns; dob and doba (instrumental i-deklinacije; imenice dob i doba)
topics.summary: Feminine i-declension nouns have two instrumental singular options: the ending -i (identical to the genitive/dative) or the fused ending -ju, which triggers sound changes at the stem boundary: sol → solju, ljubav → ljubavlju, misao → mišlju, radost → radošću, noć → noći/noću. The -ju forms are preferred when the noun stands alone without an adjective (Piše kredom i ljubavlju); with a modifier, -i is common (s velikom radosti/radošću). Two look-alike nouns must be kept apart: dob (age) is feminine i-declension (u mojoj dobi — at my age), while doba (period, season) is neuter and keeps the same form in all singular cases (u to doba — at that time, godišnje doba — season).
tables:
Instrumental sg. of i-declension (f.)
Noun      I sg. (-ju)      Sound change
--------  ---------------  -------------
sol       solju            l + j
ljubav    ljubavlju        v -> vlj
krv       krvlju           v -> vlj
misao     mišlju           sao -> šlj
radost    radošću          st -> šć
noć       noću / noći      —
stvar     stvarju / stvari —

dob (f., i-decl.)          doba (n., unchanged in sg.)
N dob    u mojoj dobi (L)  N/G/D/A/L doba
G dobi                     godišnje doba (season)
                           u to doba (at that time)

topics.title: Collective nouns (zbirne imenice)
topics.summary: Collective nouns name a group as a single mass: djeca (children), braća (brothers), gospoda (gentlemen), lišće (leaves), cvijeće (flowers), granje (branches), telad (calves), unučad (grandchildren). Grammatically they behave in a mixed way that must be learned: djeca, braća and gospoda decline like singular feminine nouns in -a (s djecom, o braći) but take a plural verb, with the participle in -la: Djeca su se igrala, Braća su došla. The -je collectives (lišće, cvijeće) are neuter singular in every respect: Lišće je palo. The -ad collectives (telad, unučad) are feminine i-declension singular: S teladi. Collectives replace the plural of some nouns entirely — dijete has no plural *dijeteta; its plural is djeca; brat's everyday plural is braća.
tables:
Collective   Meaning        Declension       Verb agreement
-----------  -------------  ---------------  -----------------
djeca        children       like žena (sg.)  plural: Djeca su
                            s djecom, djeci  se igrala.
braća        brothers       like žena (sg.)  plural: Braća su
                            s braćom, braći  došla.
gospoda      gentlemen      like žena (sg.)  plural
lišće        leaves         neuter sg.       singular: Lišće
cvijeće      flowers        neuter sg.       je palo.
granje       branches       neuter sg.       singular
telad        calves         f. i-decl. sg.   singular
unučad       grandchildren  f. i-decl. sg.   singular

dijete (sg.) -> djeca (pl.)   brat (sg.) -> braća (pl.)

topics.title: The passive participle (glagolski pridjev trpni)
topics.summary: The passive participle turns a verb into an adjective meaning 'having been X-ed' and is the key to passive sentences and countless everyday expressions (zatvoreno, rezervirano, zabranjeno). Formation follows the infinitive class: verbs in -ati take -an (napisati → napisan, pročitati → pročitan); verbs in -iti and -jeti take -en with iotation of the stem consonant (učiniti → učinjen, vidjeti → viđen, baciti → bačen); consonant-stem verbs also take -en (peći → pečen, donijeti → donesen); and a small group takes -t (uzeti → uzet, početi → počet, čuti → čut, otvoriti → otvoren but zauzeti → zauzet). The participle agrees like an adjective (pečen, pečena, pečeno) and combines with biti for the passive: Ručak je skuhan (lunch is cooked), Knjiga je napisana prije sto godina.
tables:
Ending  Class            Examples
------  ---------------  ---------------------------
-an     -ati             napisati -> napisan
                         pročitati -> pročitan
                         prodati  -> prodan
-en     -iti (iotation)  učiniti  -> učinjen
                         baciti   -> bačen
                         zatvoriti-> zatvoren
        -jeti            vidjeti  -> viđen
        consonant stems  peći     -> pečen
                         donijeti -> donesen
                         jesti    -> jeden (pojeden)
-t      -eti/-uti/short  uzeti    -> uzet
                         početi   -> počet
                         zauzeti  -> zauzet
                         čuti     -> čut

Agreement: pečen kruh, pečena riba, pečeno meso
Passive:   Večera je skuhana. Stol je rezerviran.

topics.title: The present verbal adverb (glagolski prilog sadašnji)
topics.summary: The present verbal adverb expresses an action happening at the same time as the main verb — English '-ing' clauses: Slušajući glazbu, učim (Listening to music, I study). It is formed only from imperfective verbs by adding -ći to the 3rd person plural present: čitaju → čitajući, rade → radeći, pišu → pišući, budu → budući (which also became the adjective 'future' and the conjunction budući da — 'since'). The form never changes — it does not agree with anything. In standard usage its unexpressed subject must be the same as the subject of the main clause; it belongs mostly to written and careful spoken language, where it replaces a dok-clause: Dok putujem, čitam = Putujući čitam.
tables:
3rd pl. present + -ći  ->  verbal adverb
---------------------------------------
čitaju   -> čitajući    (while) reading
rade     -> radeći      (while) working
pišu     -> pišući      (while) writing
slušaju  -> slušajući   (while) listening
putuju   -> putujući    (while) travelling
budu     -> budući      being (budući da = since)

Only from imperfective verbs; form never changes.
Same subject rule:
Putujući vlakom, čitala je novine. (she travels, she reads)

topics.title: Verbal nouns (glagolske imenice)
topics.summary: Verbal nouns name the action itself — 'reading', 'swimming' — and are formed from the passive-participle stem plus -je: čitati → čitanje, pisati → pisanje, učiti → učenje, putovati → putovanje, kupiti → kupnja/kupovanje. Most are built from imperfective verbs and are neuter nouns of the more type, declining regularly (bez čitanja, u pisanju). They are extremely productive in notices, schedules and formal style (parkiranje zabranjeno, radno vrijeme, znanje jezika), and often correspond to an English gerund or noun in '-ing'/'-tion'. Where a shorter noun exists (odluka 'decision' from odlučiti), the verbal noun in -nje still names the process rather than the result (odlučivanje 'decision-making').
tables:
Verb          Verbal noun    Meaning
------------  -------------  ------------------
čitati        čitanje        reading
pisati        pisanje        writing
učiti         učenje         learning, studying
plivati       plivanje       swimming
putovati      putovanje      travelling, trip
parkirati     parkiranje     parking
znati         znanje         knowledge
piti          piće           drink
(process vs result)
odlučiti -> odluka (result) / odlučivanje (process)
Declines as neuter: bez učenja, u putovanju, znanjem

topics.title: Subjectless sentences (besubjektne rečenice)
topics.summary: Some Croatian sentences have no subject at all, not even an implied one; the verb stands in the 3rd person singular neuter. They describe natural phenomena (Grmi — it's thundering, Sviće — dawn is breaking, Smrkava se — it's getting dark), existence and absence with nema/nije bilo/neće biti + genitive (Nema kruha — there is no bread, Nije bilo struje — there was no electricity), and states with adverbs (Kasno je, Bilo je lijepo). In the past these verbs take the neuter participle: Grmjelo je cijelu noć. The nema + genitive pattern is essential: it is how Croatian says 'there isn't/aren't', and its past and future are nije bilo and neće biti, all with the genitive.
tables:
Type              Present         Past
----------------  --------------  ------------------
weather           Grmi.           Grmjelo je.
                  Sviće.          Svanulo je.
                  Smrkava se.     Smrkavalo se.
existence (+ G!)  Ima kruha.      Bilo je kruha.
absence (+ G!)    Nema kruha.     Nije bilo kruha.
                  Nema ga.        Nije ga bilo.
     future:      Neće biti problema.
state + adverb    Kasno je.       Bilo je kasno.
                  Vruće je.       Bilo je vruće.

topics.title: Conditional sentences (pogodbene rečenice s ako, kad i da)
topics.summary: Croatian has three types of conditional sentence, each with its own conjunction pattern. Real conditions (likely, future) use ako with the present or futur II in the if-clause and futur I or an imperative in the main clause: Ako budeš učio, položit ćeš. Potential conditions (imaginable but hypothetical) use kad or ako with kondicional I in both clauses: Kad bih imao novca, kupio bih stan. Unreal conditions (contrary to fact) use da with the present or perfekt in the if-clause and kondicional I in the main clause: Da sam znao, došao bih (If I had known, I would have come — but I didn't). The choice of conjunction is itself meaningful: da + indicative always signals that the condition is not fulfilled.
tables:
Type        If-clause               Main clause
----------  ----------------------  -----------------
1. real     ako + present/futur II  futur I / imper.
            Ako budeš učio,         položit ćeš ispit.
            Ako imaš vremena,       dođi!
2. potential kad/ako + kondicional  kondicional I
            Kad bih imao novca,     kupio bih stan.
            Ako bi htio,            mogao bi doći.
3. unreal   da + present/perfekt    kondicional I
            Da sam znao,            došao bih.
            Da imam vremena,        pomogao bih ti.

da + indicative = condition NOT fulfilled
